title: Sparse Linear Networks with a Fixed Butterfly Structure: Theory and Practice

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper provides an interesting and novel use of butterfly factoziations in encoder-decoder networks. Specifically, the paper proposes replacing the 
encoder with a truncated butterfly network followed by a dense linear layer. The parameters are chosen so as to keep the number of weights in the (replaced) encoder near linear in the input dimension. The authors provide a theoretical result related to auto-encoder optimization.

######

I vote for accepting the paper. The main reason is for my vote is that the main idea introduced is novel and the algorithmic contribution is substantial. 

######

pros 
+ The proposed truncated butterfly network is novel. Aside from the algorithmic contribution, the theorem in the paper raise important questions about
the optimization landscape of butterfly networks. 
+ The paper is clearly written and well justified 
+ Exhaustive literature survey and background on relevant work in both the matrix factorization and neural networks front

######

cons 
- The beginning of section 7 needs to be expanded with more details on the original Indyk et al 2019 paper -- the authors mention that they use the same setting as 
the 2019 paper but the details on how Indyk et al train their network are lacking without looking up the original paper. 

- I think that low matrix approximation experimentation (Sec 7) can be more thorough. Why are only three datasets (in Table 3) used? Additionally and more importantly, Table 4 shows the approximation results for low values of k (max of 30) -- what happens when k=(min(n,d))? Also, the error is measured with respect to the best rank k approximation of X (the eq. following eq. 20) While this way of measuring the error is often used in the low rank matrix approximation literature it is not very informative of the actual approximation quality of \tilde X. It could well be the case that \tilde X = X_k but the error ||X-\tilde X||_F/||X||_F might  be poor hence this way of measuring the approximation performance is a better indicator of how well X is actually approximated in practice.   

######

questions: see the above section

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper presents a compelling method for replacing the encoder in encoder–decoder architectures with a truncated butterfly network followed by a dense layer, yielding a near–linear parameter count. The authors provide solid algorithmic contributions alongside theoretical analysis of the optimization landscape of these butterfly networks. The writing is concise and well structured, and the literature review thoroughly situates the work within both matrix factorization and neural network research. Overall, I find the approach novel and the theoretical insights important, and I recommend acceptance (Rating: 7: Good paper, accept), subject to a few clarifications and expanded experiments.

Strengths  
1. Novelty of the truncated butterfly network: Introducing a structured factorization in the encoder is an original idea that could have broad applicability in efficient neural architectures.  
2. Theoretical contributions: The paper’s analysis of critical points and optimization behavior in butterfly networks raises significant questions for future research on structured layers.  
3. Clarity and exposition: The manuscript is well written, with clear motivation, precise definitions, and logical flow.  
4. Comprehensive literature survey: The related work section admirably covers both classic matrix sketching/factorization techniques and recent neural-network alternatives, giving readers a full picture of the field.

Weaknesses  
1. Details on Indyk et al. (2019) setup (Sec. 7): The paper references the training procedure of Indyk et al. but does not explain how their network is instantiated or trained in this context, making it hard to judge the fairness and reproducibility of comparisons.  
2. Limited datasets in low-rank approximation experiments: Table 3 reports results on only three datasets, which may not sufficiently demonstrate robustness across data regimes.  
3. Restricted range of k in Table 4: Approximations are evaluated only for k ≤ 30, leaving out higher-rank scenarios up to min(n,d) where performance trends could differ.  
4. Choice of error metric: Using the Frobenius norm difference ‖X − X̃‖_F relative to the best rank-k approximation does not directly reflect how well X̃ reconstructs X in practice; a normalized reconstruction error might be more informative.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Expand Section 7: At the start of the experimental setup, include detailed descriptions of how Indyk et al.’s model is trained (architecture specifics, hyperparameters, optimization settings) to improve reproducibility and clarity.  
2. Broaden dataset coverage: Add more diverse matrices—varying in size, condition number, and application domain—to the low-rank sketching experiments so as to validate the method more thoroughly.  
3. Extend k values: Report approximation quality for larger k values up to min(n,d). This will demonstrate whether the truncated butterfly approach scales gracefully across the full rank spectrum.  
4. Report normalized reconstruction error: In addition to the current metric, include a measure such as ‖X − X̃‖_F/‖X‖_F (or other relative error) to better capture practical approximation fidelity.

Conclusion  
This manuscript delivers a noteworthy algorithmic idea supported by significant theoretical analysis and clear exposition. The suggested revisions—primarily clarifications in Section 7 and more extensive empirical evaluation—are quite feasible and would strengthen the empirical claims. I therefore recommend acceptance.

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes to impose a particular sparsity structure (butterfly network) to replace dense connected layers in deep neural networks. It is motivated by the theoretical results involving the Fast Johnson Lindenstrauss Transform (FJLT). The work is well motivated and experimental results validate the theoretical findings. However, it is not clear the advantage for the case of image datasets as CIFAR10 and CIFAR100. I have the following comments and questions that should be clarified to evaluate the relevance of the results:
-	When comparing with other architectures on image classification tasks (CIFAR10 and CIFAR 100), what is the dense layer that is replaced by the butterfly structure? Most very well-known architectures are based on large concatenation of convolutional layers with a dense layer at the end. Is this last layer the one that is replaced? What is the compression attained by this replacement? 
-	In this architecture, the sparsity pattern is fixed, and it seems to work very well for the dataset used in the paper. It would be interesting to provide some insights on why this particularly structure works well on natural data. Is there any property on datasets that makes butterfly pattern optimal?
-	For natural images, it is well known that sparsity structure imposed by convolutional layers is optimal because of local structure of natural images. I think a comparison replacing a convolutional layer by a butterfly layer could bring some useful insights.
-	Theorem 1: What is Omega in the exponent? I couldn’t find its definition.
-	Figure 1(a): The small difference between normal and BF models seems not to be statistically significant. It would be good to show some variability of results (error bars).
-	 For the results of Figure 1(b), it would be also useful to report the training time reduction and model compression attained by the normal and BF models.
-	Figure 1(b): The starting point for the four methods should be the same. Please include in the plot the Test Accuracy at the beginning (epoch 0).
-	Figure 1(b): Please extend the plots beyond epoch 20 to see how the values are stabilized for the four methods.
-	Quality of Figures, for example Fig 3, must be improved (too small fonts, blurred, etc.)

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 3: The reviewer is fairly confident that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
I list the strong and weak points in the following:

### Strong points

- It is a novel idea (only to my knowledge) to combine the butterfly network and FJLT to optimize the neural network architectures. Specifically, the proposed method replaces a dense linear layer by a composition of three layers, which are smaller and can be computed faster by FJLT. 


### Weak points

- The paper says that the proposed method would speed up the training. But there is no quantitative argument. Also the paper does not report the computing time or memory used in all the experiments.

- In Definition 4.1, $n$ is required to be a power of 2. And in the experiments, it seems that all the $n$ are powers of 2 (except Tech). What do one do if $n$ is not a power of 2? 

- In all the experiments, the final hidden layer is replaced by the proposed architecture. Is there any reason for doing this? How should one choose which layer(s) to be replaced?

- It is argued that by replacing a dense layer with a truncated butterfly network and a dense layer, the number of parameters is reduced from $kn$ to $k\ell+O(n\log\ell)$. But since the Big O can hide potentially large constants, I wonder how many parameters are used exactly. I expect the paper would report the parameter numbers in the experiments. 

- Theorem 1 shows that replacing a dense layer by two truncated butterfly networks and a dense layer will not be too different from the original network. But this holds only when $J_1$ and $J_2$ are sampled from the FJLT distribution. Since the weights $J_1$ and $J_2$ are updated through the training process, the theorem will only hold at initialization. Then what is the point of Theorem 1?

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review for “Butterfly Networks with Fast Johnson–Lindenstrauss Transforms for Efficient Layer Replacement”

Summary  
This paper proposes to replace a traditional dense linear layer with a sequence of three smaller layers parameterized via butterfly network structures and Fast Johnson–Lindenstrauss Transforms (FJLT). The authors claim that this integration yields significant savings in parameter count—dropping from kn down to kℓ + O(n log ℓ)—while preserving accuracy, and suggest practical benefits in training and inference speed. Although the idea of marrying structured transforms with neural architectures is intriguing, the manuscript falls short in providing the empirical and theoretical clarity needed to justify its claims.

Strengths  
1. Novelty of approach: The combination of butterfly network parametrizations with random FJLT projections to approximate dense transforms is original and promising.  
2. Architectural contribution: By decomposing a dense layer into three FJLT-computable components, the paper offers a concrete instantiation that could reduce memory footprint and potentially improve computational efficiency in deep networks.

Weaknesses  
1. Missing quantitative benchmarks: The paper does not report any concrete measurements of training time, inference latency, or memory consumption. Claims of “faster and smaller” remain qualitative, making it difficult to assess practical impact.  
2. Restrictive dimensionality assumption: Definition 4.1 requires the input dimension n to be a power of two. Aside from a single “Tech” dataset experiment, there is no discussion on extending this to arbitrary n, which limits applicability in real‐world scenarios.  
3. Unjustified layer selection: All experiments only replace the final hidden layer with the butterfly+FJLT block. The manuscript offers no rationale or guidelines for why this layer was chosen, nor whether earlier or multiple layers could benefit similarly.  
4. O-notation hides constants: The key parameter reduction bound kn → kℓ + O(n log ℓ) is stated without concrete counts. Hidden constants in the O(·) term may nullify practical savings, but the paper does not report exact parameter tallies used in experiments.  
5. Theoretical guarantee limited to initialization: Theorem 1 assumes that the FJLT matrices J₁ and J₂ remain fixed samples, yet in practice these and the butterfly weights are jointly updated during training. Thus, the stated approximation guarantee does not hold beyond model initialization.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Empirical benchmarking: Provide a table of wall-clock training times, per‐step GPU memory usage, and inference latencies comparing standard dense layers to the proposed replacement across multiple architectures and datasets.  
2. Handling non–power-of-2 inputs: Describe strategies (e.g., padding, truncation, or alternate transforms) to accommodate arbitrary input sizes, and demonstrate their effect in an ablation study.  
3. Layer replacement guidelines: Offer criteria—such as layer width, depth, or sensitivity—for selecting which layers to substitute with the butterfly+FJLT block. Experiments on replacing earlier or multiple layers would strengthen the practical message.  
4. Exact parameter counts: Report the full parameter budget for both baseline and proposed models (breaking down kn, kℓ, and the hidden constant factors in O(n log ℓ)). This will clarify true model-size reductions.  
5. Extend or contextualize Theorem 1: Either adjust the theorem to account for post-initialization updates of J₁ and J₂ or explicitly discuss how empirical performance remains aligned with the theoretical bound despite training dynamics.

Conclusion  
The paper introduces a clever structural shortcut for dense layers via butterfly networks and FJLT, and the core idea is of clear interest to the community. However, the absence of quantitative results, the restrictive power-of-2 assumption, lack of justification for layer choice, opaque parameter counts, and the limited scope of the theoretical guarantee all weaken the submission. As it stands, the work requires substantial empirical and theoretical reinforcement before it can be recommended for publication.

Rating: 5 – Marginally below acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper studies “butterfly networks”, where, a logarithmic number of linear layers with sparse connections resembling the butterfly structure of the FFT algorithm, along with linear layers in smaller dimensions are used to approximate linear layers in larger dimensions. In general, the paper follows the idea of sketching to design new architectures that can reduce the number of trainable parameters.  In that regard, the paper is very appealing, as it shows that replacing linear layers with the butterfly networks does not result in any loss in performance. 

The paper’s aim is to establish that linear layers can be replaced by butterfly networks and uses three different experiments to show this. The experiments do lend evidence to the claim that butterfly networks do not lead to a loss in performance. 

The paper also present some theoretical results to show that the using butterfly networks sampled from the FJLT family preserves certain metric. However, my opinion is that Theorems 1 and 2 are mostly direct consequences of existing theorems in the sketching literature.

Finally, although I appreciate the experiments in the paper, I feel strongly that the paper lacks motivation. The idea of sketching and using FJLT transforms have had a great impact on numerical linear algebra and generally in reducing computational complexity of algorithms. However, it is unclear from this paper what the expected impact is. Probably the authors could focus more on this. Possible directions are to show improved training times, or maybe even showing that such networks are more resilient towards overfitting. 

As interesting as the experiments are, the paper needs a better motivation and further exploration of the utility of the ideas presented. For example, can using butterfly networks improve theory or practice of deep learning/machine learning? I do like the paper, but the paper can be much stronger and much more appealing if the motivation is better justified.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper investigates the use of butterfly‐structured sketching layers—drawn from the Fast Johnson–Lindenstrauss Transform (FJLT) family—as a drop-in replacement for standard dense linear layers in neural networks. The authors propose decomposing a full dense layer into a sequence of sparse butterfly transforms followed by smaller, dense projections. They present two theorems on metric preservation (in the spirit of FJLT) and empirically validate their approach on three distinct experimental setups, showing that accuracy remains on par with the original dense architectures.

Strengths  
1. The idea of leveraging sketching techniques—specifically butterfly networks—to cut down the number of trainable parameters is both elegant and practically relevant.  
2. The paper includes three thorough experiments comparing standard dense layers against the proposed butterfly‐based layers, consistently demonstrating no degradation in performance.  
3. The empirical results convincingly show that the compressed architectures match the original networks in terms of final accuracy, suggesting a viable path for parameter-efficient model design.

Weaknesses  
1. The theoretical contributions (Theorems 1 and 2) largely restate well‐known metric‐preservation properties from the sketching literature, offering limited novelty.  
2. The manuscript does not provide a clear, compelling motivation for why replacing dense layers with butterfly structures will have a significant impact on deep learning practice.  
3. It remains unclear what practical benefits—beyond parameter count reduction—these architectures yield, such as faster training, reduced memory bandwidth, or improved robustness to overfitting.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Articulate a stronger motivation and discuss the potential real‐world impact of butterfly layers in typical deep learning pipelines (e.g., inference speedups, memory savings on edge devices).  
2. Augment the empirical evaluation with secondary metrics, such as end‐to‐end training time, convergence speed, or generalization under limited data, to demonstrate benefits beyond parameter compression.  
3. Investigate additional theoretical or practical advantages—perhaps exploring stability properties, robustness to noise, or scalability to larger models—to broaden the paper’s appeal and technical depth.

Decision  
While the experimental results are solid and the application of FJLT‐style sketching to neural architectures is appealing, the theoretical analysis offers limited novelty and the paper lacks a persuasive discussion of broader impact. Thus I recommend “5: Marginally below acceptance threshold.”

title: Variational Structured Attention Networks for Dense Pixel-Wise Prediction

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes a novel structure attention mechanism with a probabilistic CRF-like inference framework for the pixel-wise prediction, which not only models the spatial-wise dependencies but also considers the channel-level dependencies using an attention tensor. This attention mechanism is obviously different from the existing attention mechanism. Additionally, many quantitative and qualitative experimental results on three pixel-wise prediction tasks have demonstrated the superiority of the proposed method. The proposed attention mechanism is useful and flexible, which could be integrated into any pixel-wise prediction frameworks in theory. However, I think there exist some disadvantages:

1. There are some unclear statements in the paper. For instance, the authors say “the attention tensor is nothing but the sum of T rank-1 tensors”, but how are these rank-1 tensors generated by the spatial attention map and the channel-wise attention vector? Is each rank-1 tensor associated with the special regions of different objects? If the authors visualize the attention map, it would be better to understand. Moreover, the overall architecture of the proposed method is not clear. The authors do not specify the details of the multi-scale features, including the dimensions of them, where they come from, etc. The dimensions of the most important variables are also not specified, which makes the paper is hard to follow.

2. In the ablation study, when the model is ‘no structure’ on a single scale, does it denote the model does not integrate any attention mechanism? If so, when only considering the spatial or channel attention, why does it outperform DANet that includes dual attentions? If not, I think the comparison is not fair. 

3. Although the performance of the proposed attention mechanism outperforms most existing methods, the authors do not report the runtimes and parameters. Moreover, whether does it significantly burden the complexity of the model with the increase of T?

4. There are some grammar mistakes and typos. For example, “Since the exact a posteriori distribution is not computationally tractable, ...”, “Again, our method not outperforms …”

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a novel structured attention mechanism formulated as a sum of T rank-1 tensors within a probabilistic, CRF-like variational inference framework. By jointly modeling spatial and channel dependencies through an attention tensor, the proposed approach extends beyond traditional attention modules. The authors validate their model on three pixel-wise prediction tasks—depth estimation, surface normal prediction, and semantic segmentation—demonstrating consistent quantitative and qualitative improvements over strong baselines. Overall, the idea is compelling and shows promise, but several presentation and experimental details need clarification before the work can be fully appreciated and reproduced.

Strengths  
1. Novelty of the attention formulation: The core idea of expressing structured attention as the sum of T rank-1 tensors is original and well motivated.  
2. Joint spatial-channel modeling: Unlike many existing methods that treat spatial and channel attentions independently, this work integrates them in a single tensorial framework.  
3. Clear differentiation from prior art: The manuscript does a good job contrasting its probabilistic inference approach with standard self-attention and dual attention networks (e.g., DANet).  
4. Strong empirical performance: Extensive experiments on multiple datasets and tasks consistently show gains over competitive baselines. Both quantitative metrics and qualitative visualizations support the effectiveness of the method.  
5. Broad applicability: The proposed module is sufficiently general to be plugged into various pixel-level prediction architectures, which enhances its practical value.

Weaknesses  
1. Tensor construction is unclear: The process by which each rank-1 tensor is derived from a spatial attention map mt and a channel vector vt is not described in sufficient detail. Readers are left uncertain about the exact operations and dimensions involved.  
2. Vague architecture description: The paper lacks specifics regarding the multi-scale feature streams—where they originate in the backbone, their spatial resolutions, and channel dimensionalities.  
3. Ambiguous ablation settings: The definition of the “no structure” ablation, particularly when applied at a single scale, does not seem comparable to the full multi-scale or to DANet. This raises questions about the fairness of the baseline comparisons.  
4. Missing complexity analysis: There is no reporting of runtime performance (e.g., frames per second), total parameter count, or how computational cost grows as the tensor rank T increases.  
5. Language and grammar issues: Multiple typos and awkward phrasings (for example, “Again, our method not outperforms …”) detract from the readability and professionalism of the manuscript.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Visualize tensor components: Include example attention maps mt, channel vectors vt, and their outer products to illustrate how each rank-1 tensor contributes to the final attention tensor.  
2. Detail the backbone and multi-scale setup: Specify which layers of the feature extractor are used, their spatial sizes, and channel dimensions. A table summarizing these details would greatly improve clarity.  
3. Clarify ablation definitions: Precisely define each ablation variant (especially “no structure”) and ensure that comparisons to baselines like DANet are made on equal footing (same scales, parameter budgets, etc.).  
4. Report and analyze complexity: Provide run-time benchmarks, total parameter counts, and a study showing how inference time and memory usage scale with different tensor ranks T.  
5. Proofread carefully: Conduct a thorough language edit to correct grammatical errors and typos, ensuring the narrative reads smoothly.

Minor Comments  
- In Section 3.2, please define all notation used in the tensor decomposition step before introducing equations.  
- Consider adding a brief discussion about potential failure cases or limitations when T is set too low or too high.  
- Ensure all figures are referenced in the text and include clear captions explaining what is being shown.

Recommendation: Marginally Above Acceptance Threshold (Rating: 6)  
The paper presents a creative and impactful extension to structured attention mechanisms, backed by strong empirical results. However, clarity of presentation, fair ablation design, and complexity reporting must be addressed to solidify the contribution. With these improvements, I believe the work would make a valuable addition to the conference.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Overview

This paper carefully designs a structured attention network incorporating with a variational solution, to benefit the dense pixel-wise prediction via inferring the latent attention gate between spatial and channel-level features. Based on the suppose of T rank-1 attention tensors, the proposed structured attention network performs a CRF formulation with latent gating variables. 

Strengths

1.	Experiments are conducted on various dataset KITTI and NYU-v2 for depth estimation, PASCAL and Cityscapes for semantic segmentation, ScanNet for surface normal prediction. All the results demonstrate the outperformance.

2.	The motivation is clear. Jointly estimating spatial attention maps and channel attention vectors with a probabilistic framework.

Weakness

1.	The contribution is limited. Just a combination of two existing works, e.g., CRF-based models for multi-scale attention estimation and DANet for spatial and channel-wise attention. Why the T rank attention tensors are advanced than full-rank ones? How to guarantee the T channels optimal than the possible C?

2.	The generation of structured latent attention gate is confused. Compared with the work (Xu et al., 2017a), which illustrates the attention-gated CRFs with a clear graph, Figure 1 confuses me. Where is the gate? How to produce a probabilistically enhanced feature map? 

3.	The model is complex with four sets of latent variables (Z, M, V, and K) to be inferred. It is hard to be reproduced.

Questions:

1.	How to choose the parameter T? Ablation study might be added to demonstrate the difference with different T.

2.	In the inference stage, there are Z-step, M-step, V-step, and K-step. How to deal with so many variables to obtain the optimal performance? Is there any dependence on data size?

3.	For experiments of depth estimation, why the proposed method obtains optimal metrics but rel?

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review Summary  
This paper proposes a probabilistic framework that jointly estimates spatial attention maps and channel attention vectors for dense prediction tasks. The authors integrate a conditional random field (CRF)-style multi-scale attention mechanism with separate spatial/channel attention modules, yielding strong empirical results on depth and semantic segmentation benchmarks (KITTI, NYU-v2, PASCAL, Cityscapes, ScanNet). While the motivation is clearly laid out and the experimental evaluation is comprehensive, the technical novelty is limited, the structured latent attention gate is not sufficiently explained, and the inference complexity raises reproducibility concerns.

Strengths  
1. Thorough Experiments: The method is evaluated on five diverse datasets, demonstrating consistent improvements over competitive baselines. Results on most standard metrics are strong, showcasing the practical value of the joint attention modeling.  
2. Well-Motivated Framework: The paper clearly motivates why spatial and channel attention should be estimated jointly in a probabilistic setting, building upon CRF-based multi-scale reasoning to capture richer context.

Weaknesses  
1. Limited Novelty: The core idea largely combines existing CRF-based multi-scale attention with DANet-style spatial and channel attention. There is no fundamentally new insight beyond integrating these components.  
2. Unclear Gate Formulation: The role and formulation of the structured latent attention gate (with variables Z, M, V, K) are not transparent. Figure 1 does not clearly depict where the gate operates nor how it transforms features into probabilistically weighted maps.  
3. High Inference Complexity: Introducing four sets of latent variables complicates training and inference. Without clear algorithmic details, reproducing the Z-step, M-step, V-step, and K-step updates is difficult, especially given potential dependencies on dataset size and hyperparameters.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Ablation on Tensor Rank T: Include experiments that vary the low-rank tensor parameter T, comparing full-rank versus low-rank settings, to justify the chosen value of T and to illustrate its impact on performance and efficiency.  
2. Clarify the Latent Gate Mechanism: Provide an additional diagram or flow graph that explicitly shows how the attention gate processes feature maps into probabilistically enhanced outputs. Annotate where each latent variable contributes.  
3. Detail the Inference Procedure: Add a pseudo-code or step-by-step description of the Z-step, M-step, V-step, and K-step, specifying any dataset-size dependencies or convergence criteria. This will greatly aid reproducibility.  
4. Explain Depth-Error Behavior: Although the method excels on most depth metrics, it underperforms on the relative error (rel). A brief analysis or hypothesis for this discrepancy would strengthen the experimental section.

Additional Comments  
- The paper’s contribution should be reframed to emphasize integration challenges rather than suggesting a wholly novel attention paradigm.  
- Justification for selecting T channels out of C should go beyond computational savings, perhaps discussing representational trade-offs.  
- If space permits, consider a small table listing the computational complexity (FLOPs or runtime) for each inference step.

Recommendation  
Rating: 6 (Marginally above acceptance threshold)  
Despite its incremental nature and some clarity issues, the paper delivers solid empirical gains and a clear motivation for joint probabilistic attention. Addressing the above concerns—particularly the gate formulation and inference details—would elevate reproducibility and deepen the technical contribution. I recommend marginal acceptance.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes a unified method to combine spatial and channel attention in a probabilistic framework, so that spatial and channel attention weights and probabilistic variables can be jointly optimized. The proposed method is incoporated in the proposed VISTA-Net to achieve state of the art performance in two dense pixel-wise prediction tasks: monocular depth prediction, semantic segmentation.

Overall this work is well motivated and organized in a good shape, so that I am on the positive side. I have some concerns as listed below.

1. My main concern is that this paper combines ideas of (Fu et al., 2019) and (Xu etal., 2017a), i.e., combining spatial and channel attention from (Fu et al., 2019) and Attention-Gated CRFs from (Xu etal., 2017a). 

2. In Eq.1, the attention tensor is limited to T rank. However, it is not clear to me how this can faciliate the inference in Eq.9.

3. In Eq.2, this paper proposes to additionally model CRF kernels. However, it is not explained why this is necessary. This is important as it is listed as a difference to existing method (Xu etal., 2017a). 

4. The approximation used in Eq.3 needs more details or reference to support.


Minor issues.
1. There are a lot of symbols in section 2. It would be better to draw a figure to include these symbols, while illustrating how the CRF formulation is incorporated in the attention mechanism.
2. The joint learning in section 2.2 can be formed in a algorithm procedure, so that others could see it more clearly.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper introduces a unified probabilistic framework that integrates spatial and channel attention within a conditional random field (CRF) model and jointly learns both attention weights and probabilistic variables. The approach is instantiated in a network called VISTA-Net and demonstrates strong empirical performance on monocular depth estimation and semantic segmentation benchmarks. The work is well motivated, clearly organized, and shows compelling state-of-the-art results in dense pixel-wise prediction tasks.

Strengths  
1. The proposed model unifies spatial and channel attention under a single probabilistic formulation, offering a coherent view of attention mechanisms.  
2. The framework jointly optimizes attention weights and latent probabilistic variables, rather than treating them in isolation.  
3. Integration into VISTA-Net yields excellent empirical improvements on two challenging tasks, confirming the practical value of the method.  
4. Experimental results are thorough, with comparisons demonstrating competitive or superior performance to existing baselines.  
5. The overall presentation is well motivated, with a logical flow from motivation through experiments.

Weaknesses  
1. Novelty: The core idea essentially merges separate spatial/channel attention (Fu et al. 2019) with attention-gated CRFs (Xu et al. 2017a), raising questions about the extent of new contributions.  
2. Equation 1 introduces a low-rank attention tensor (sum of T rank-1 terms), but it is not shown how this constraint facilitates the inference update in Equation 9.  
3. The rationale for modeling and inferring CRF kernel variables in Equation 2 is not sufficiently justified, nor is it clearly distinguished from prior CRF-based attention work.  
4. The variational approximation in Equation 3 is stated without detailed derivation or citation of supporting references.  
5. Section 2 employs a dense set of symbols with no guiding illustration, which hampers readability.  
6. The joint learning and inference procedure in Section 2.2 is described textually but would benefit from an explicit algorithmic presentation.

Suggestions for Improvement  
– Provide a clear derivation or intuitive explanation of why the rank-T constraint in Eq. 1 improves or simplifies the inference update in Eq. 9.  
– Elaborate on the motivation for including CRF kernel variables in Eq. 2, and clarify how this extension differs from Xu et al. (2017a).  
– Augment the description of the variational approximation (Eq. 3) with either a more detailed derivation or citations to standard variational inference literature.  
– Introduce a schematic figure in Section 2 that defines all key symbols and visually illustrates the CRF-based attention mechanism and factorization.  
– Summarize the joint learning and inference steps from Section 2.2 in a step-by-step algorithm box to enhance clarity.

Recommendation  
Overall, this paper makes a meaningful contribution by unifying attention mechanisms in a probabilistic CRF framework and demonstrating strong results in practice. While the methodological novelty is somewhat incremental and the exposition could be clearer in key areas, these issues can be addressed with relatively minor revisions. I therefore recommend acceptance (Rating: 6 – marginally above threshold).

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
##########################################################################

Summary:

This paper proposes the VarIational STructured Attention networks (VISTA-Net), which improves pervious SOTA models for dense pixel-wise prediction tasks. The proposed VISTA-Net is featured by two aspects: 1) A new structured attention is proposed, which is able to jointly model spatial-level and channel-level dependencies; 2) It incorporates the proposed structured attention with a CRF-like inference framework, which allows the probabilistic inference. Experimental studies are conducted on monocular depth estimation and semantic image segmentation, showing improved performances of VISTA-Net consistently.

##########################################################################

Reasons for score:

Overall, I vote for rejection. My major concerns lie in three aspects, as detailed in Cons below: 1) This work is highly similar to Xu et al. (2017a) in terms of both methods and presentations. The difference is not significant; 2) While the presentation mainly follows Xu et al. (2017a), it needs some improvement; 3) The experimental studies lack more detailed analysis on the proposed method.

##########################################################################

Pros: 

1. The work is well-motivated. The aim of the proposed method sounds natural to me.

2. I like the ablation studies. But they could be performed on at least one more dataset.

##########################################################################

Cons: 

1. This work is highly similar to Xu et al. (2017a) in terms of both methods and presentations. The difference is not significant.
Method-wise, as discussed in Related Work, the difference only lies in that VISTA-Net takes channel-level dependencies into consideration. First, this means that "Moreover, we integrate the estimation of the attention within a probabilistic framework" (quoted from abstract) is not a novel contribution. Second, considering channel-level dependencies in attention has limited novelty. As discussed in Related Work, multiple studies have explored several ways. In addition, a key step in the proposed method is Equation (1), where the tensor multiplication operator is not explained. In my understanding, it should be the outer product, or more generally, Kronecker product. Missing the clear definition of this operator hinders the clarity in describing the proposed method.
Presentation-wise, the similarity is even higher. The entire section 2 follows the exact organization of section 2 in Xu et al. (2017a). By comparing the equations and presentations, it's more convincing that the novelty of this work is quite limited.

2. While the presentation mainly follows Xu et al. (2017a), it needs some improvement. First, the same notations as in Xu et al. (2017a) are used. However, some key things are not well explained. For example, the set of hidden variables $z_s$ corresponding to $f_s$ comes out from nowhere. I have to resort to Xu et al. (2017a) to know why we need $z_s$. Second, as mentioned above, the key steps like Equation (1) lack clear explanations.

3. The experimental studies lack more detailed analysis on the proposed method. It would be more meaningful to visualize those attention maps/gates instead of dense prediction results.

##########################################################################

Questions during rebuttal period: 

Please address and clarify the cons above

##########################################################################

Comments after the rebuttal period:

Pros:

First, it is totally acceptable to follow the notations and organization of Xu et al. (2017a), as long as the statements are clear and self-contained. The original submission failed on providing key details. The authors have made revisions to address this concern. Thanks!

Second, I appreciate the extra experimental results and visualizations.

Cons:

However, the authors' responses do not fully address my concerns about the novelty, especially method-wise. I will raise my score to 5, but still recommend rejection.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents VISTA-Net, a structured attention mechanism for dense pixel-wise prediction tasks. The authors frame attention as a conditional random field (CRF) over feature channels, arguing that capturing channel-level dependencies yields more expressive attention maps. Experiments include ablation studies showing the impact of individual components.

Strengths  
• The formulation of structured attention within a probabilistic CRF framework is well motivated and aligns with prior work on energy-based models for vision.  
• Ablation studies are provided, isolating the contributions of the proposed gating variables and structured term.

Weaknesses  
• Novelty is marginal relative to Xu et al. (2017a). That work already employs a probabilistic CRF for attention, and the current extension to channel-level dependencies feels incremental.  
• Presentation closely mirrors Xu et al. (2017a) in structure and notation, which undermines self-contained clarity and makes it difficult to assess the unique contributions of VISTA-Net.  
• Key mathematical components are not fully explained. In particular, the tensor multiplication operator in Equation (1) (e.g. outer vs. Kronecker product) is never defined, and hidden variables z_s appear without justification or interpretation.  
• Notation for the channel-wise vector v_t and spatial map m_t is reused from prior work without new explanation, further harming readability.  
• The experimental section lacks depth. There are no visualizations of the learned attention maps or gating variables to support the empirical claims, and all results are reported on a single dataset, limiting confidence in generality.

Suggestions for Improvement  
1. Clearly define the tensor multiplication operator used in Equation (1), specifying whether it is an outer product, Kronecker product, or another form of tensor contraction.  
2. Provide a self-contained explanation of all variables and notation—especially the origin and role of the hidden variables z_s associated with feature maps.  
3. Include qualitative visualizations of the attention maps or gating variables across different samples to demonstrate that the structured term yields interpretable or qualitatively improved focus.  
4. Strengthen the empirical evaluation by extending experiments or ablation analyses to at least one additional dataset or task, thereby validating the robustness of the proposed components.

Decision  
Rating: 5 – Marginally below acceptance threshold. Although VISTA-Net is well motivated and supported by ablations, its core contributions are incremental over Xu et al. (2017a), and the presentation and experimental analysis require substantial improvement before the work can be considered for acceptance.
